pages.raw_text:
COLFAX, Wash, May 5.-There is
no doubt that serious damage has
been done to fruit on the uplands and
that vegetables have been injured by
the heavy frost of the past few
nights, when ice was formed in many
places. ]. r. Lee, a fruit grower of
Penewawa. is in town today and says
that matoes. potatoes and beans
have been killed by the frosts. The
vines were killed and there seems
little hope of recovery from the
freeze Mr. Lee says that tomatoes
and beans will be replanted, and it
is not too late now for a fairly good
crop, with favorable weather condi-
tons Fruit along the Snake river
Is said to have escaped injury.


Reports from the upland districts
show that serious damage has been
done to cherries, pears, plums and
other fruits, and some farmers de-
clare even apples have been injured


Such cold weather at this time of
year Is almost unprecedented and
there seems little doubt that the fruit
crop of the country will be cut short


There was heavy damage In the
upper Snake and Clearwater valleys
by the cold wave of February, and
much of the fruit crop was killed
and high prices are predicted. Fruit
growers along the Snake river in
whitman, Garfield and Columbia
counties say that buyers are securing
the country and trying to contract
their crops for delivery as soon as
the various fruits are In season. and
that good prices are being offered.
Many growers have refused to con-
/ tract their crops.
